planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; oriental cherry is the species from which most Japanese ornamental cherry cultivars are derived, including 'Kwanzan', 'Shirofugen', and 'Ukon'. Grafted cultivars are most commonly available.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; oriental cherry (Japanese flowering cherry) is the parent species of dozens of the most famous ornamental cherry cultivars in the world, including the double-flowered 'Kwanzan' (dark pink), 'Shirofugen' (white fading to pink), and 'Ukon' (pale yellow-green). In Japan, cherry blossom viewing (hanami) centered on this species and its cultivars is a centuries-old cultural tradition celebrating the brief but spectacular spring bloom. Most widely planted ornamental cherries in American gardens are cultivars derived from P. serrulata. The double-flowered cultivars produce no fruit (the extra petals replace stamens), limiting wildlife value, but the spectacle of spring bloom — covering the bare branches in clouds of pink or white — makes them among the most celebrated ornamental trees worldwide. Short-lived compared to some other ornamentals (25–30 years).

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; double flowering plum blooms in early spring before the leaves. Prune immediately after flowering to encourage vigorous new growth. The double-flowered cultivar 'Multiplex' is most widely available.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; double flowering plum is a Chinese shrub producing double-flowered, rosy-pink blooms so profusely that stems are completely hidden under flowers in very early spring. The popular cultivar 'Multiplex' has fully double, deep rose-pink flowers with a ruffled, peony-like form. This is one of the most spectacularly spring-flowering woody plants for cold climates — reliably cold-hardy to zone 3 with outstanding early season color. Often confused with double flowering almond (Prunus glandulosa) in the garden trade; both are excellent ornamental spring shrubs. The flowers appear before leaves, making the bare-stem flower display especially dramatic. Prune hard immediately after bloom to maintain compact shape and maximize next year's flowering. Short-lived (10–20 years) but easy to propagate from cuttings.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, deep, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral riparian soil in full sun; black cottonwood is the largest native hardwood tree of western North America and grows along mountain rivers and streams from Alaska to California. Plant only where ample moisture is available.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to wet, deep, well-drained riparian soil in full sun along streams and rivers; black cottonwood is the largest native hardwood of western North America, reaching 100+ feet in the riparian forests of the Pacific Coast and Cascades. The deeply furrowed, dark gray bark gives the tree its common name. The buds are large and resinous with a balsamic fragrance similar to balsam poplar. Black cottonwood riparian forests are among the most productive wildlife habitats in the western US — supporting bald eagle nesting, great blue heron colonies, beaver activity, and a rich understory of native shrubs and wildflowers. The brilliant golden-yellow fall color marks every river valley from the Cascades to the Rocky Mountains with spectacular seasonal color visible for miles. The first tree species to have its complete genome sequenced (2006), serving as the model organism for forest tree genomics.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky calcareous or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; bustic grows naturally in tropical hammocks and rockland habitats of south Florida and the Keys, tolerating thin, rocky, alkaline oolitic limestone soils well.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; bustic (false mastic) is a medium to large evergreen tree native to the tropical hammocks and rockland forests of south Florida and the Caribbean, producing a dense, spreading canopy of large, glossy, leathery leaves. The small, white, fragrant flowers are produced throughout the year in clusters, followed by small, yellow to orange edible fruits with a sweet, apricot-mango-like flavor. The fruits are eaten by white-crowned pigeons, gray catbirds, and numerous other birds as a critical food source in the south Florida hammock ecosystem. The wood is extremely hard and durable — historically used for railroad ties and boat building. A superb shade tree for south Florida native landscapes, providing an evergreen canopy, wildlife food, and attractive flowers. One of the characteristic dominant trees of the endangered Miami rock ridge hammock ecosystem.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; gold tree blooms spectacularly for 1–2 weeks before and just as the leaves emerge in late winter/early spring — the entire tree covered in brilliant golden-yellow trumpet flowers with no leaves visible. One of the most spectacular flowering trees in the world.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in frost-free zone 10–12; the gold tree (golden trumpet tree) produces one of the most spectacular flowering displays of any tree in the world — for 1–2 weeks in late winter or early spring, the entire tree is covered in blazing, golden-yellow trumpet flowers before a single leaf has emerged, creating a vision of solid gold visible from hundreds of feet away. The display lasts only 1–2 weeks and is immediately followed by the production of deeply cut, palmate compound leaves. The species is the national tree of Brazil and widely planted as a street and specimen tree throughout the tropics. Drought-tolerant once established, growing naturally in deciduous tropical dry forest. In south Florida (zone 10), it produces its spectacular bloom in February-March — one of the great floral spectacles of the Miami-Fort Lauderdale area.
